citation: Lauck BJ, Sinnott AM, Kiefer AW, Padua DA, Powell JR, Sledge HR, Mihalik JP. Association Between Head Impact Biomechanics and Physical Load in College Football. Annals of Biomedical Engineering. 2022.
abstract: This prospective study examined whether an association exists between repetitive head impact exposure (RHIE) and physical load metrics in college football across the 2017–2021 seasons (excluding 2020). Forty-one Division I players were instrumented with both the Head Impact Telemetry (HIT) System (Riddell helmets) to measure linear and rotational head accelerations and Catapult Vector GPS units embedded in halters to quantify player load. Generalized linear mixed models and linear regression models were used to analyze in-session and season-long associations while controlling for position group (BIGS, BIG SKILL, SKILL) and session type (practice vs. game). Player load was significantly associated with RHIE both within individual sessions and across an entire season, with season-long player load explaining 31% of the variability in cumulative RHIE (R²=0.31; p<0.0001). Game sessions produced significantly greater player load and RHIE compared to practice sessions, while position group did not significantly affect RHIE.
tldr: Physical load burden—as measured by GPS-based player load—is a significant predictor of head impact exposure in college football, suggesting that tracking physical load may serve as a practical proxy for anticipating repetitive head impact risk both within sessions and across a full season.
methods: Prospective cohort study; 41 male Division I college football players across four seasons (2017–2019, 2021); Head Impact Telemetry (HIT) System in Riddell helmets for head impact biomechanics; Catapult Vector GPS monitoring system in halters for player load; session RHIE computed via Stemper et al. risk-weighted exposure (RWE) formula; generalized linear mixed models (PROC MIXED) for in-session and season-long associations; linear regression (PROC REG) for secondary analyses; position groups: BIGS (OL/DL), BIG SKILL (LB/TE), SKILL (DB/RB/WR); outliers ≥3 SD removed; QBs, kickers, and punters excluded; 2020 excluded due to COVID-19.
findings:
• Player load was significantly associated with in-session RHIE (p<0.001)
• Season-long player load predicted season-long RHIE (R²=0.31; F=18.79; p<0.0001)
• Game player load and RHIE were both significantly greater than practice (p<0.001)
• Position group significantly affected in-session player load (p=0.025; SKILL > BIGS) but not RHIE (p=0.343)
• Session type significantly affected both player load and RHIE (p<0.0001), with games > practices
• 41 players contributed 2,330 unique sessions with 27,349 total head impacts recorded
• Cumulative RHIE: Game=1.1±1.3; Practice=1.4±2.0; Combined=2.5±2.8
limitations: Single college football program limits generalizability; HIT System detects ~70% of on-field head impacts; Catapult can underestimate loads by up to 15%; participants' playing status may have changed during the study; site-specific coaching strategies may influence findings; HIT System only compatible with select Riddell helmet models; QBs and specialists excluded; 2020 season excluded.
practicalImplications: Physical load tracking via GPS (already pervasive across college athletics) may serve as a real-time proxy for monitoring anticipated head impact burden. Sports medicine providers and S&C staff can use player load data to inform practice planning and scheduling to reduce cumulative head impact exposure. This approach is particularly actionable during preseason and regular season management for athletes returning from concussion or musculoskeletal injury.
athleteDev: Directly applicable to American college football. SKILL position players (DBs, WRs, RBs) generate higher player loads than BIGS (OL/DL), while linemen tend to sustain higher head impact frequency—these position-specific differences should inform individualized load management strategies. Monitoring GPS-derived player load across preseason and regular season can help coaching and medical staff calibrate practice intensity to manage cumulative head impact risk.
rtp: Physical load data may provide clinicians with a practical metric for gauging head impact re-exposure risk when returning athletes to full participation after concussion or musculoskeletal injury. Authors note findings apply directly to medical providers monitoring athletes returning to sport, as physical load burden can inform anticipated RHIE during progressive RTP protocols.

citation: Kercher KA, Steinfeldt JA, Rettke DJ, Zuidema TR, Walker MJ, Martinez Kercher VM, Silveyra P, Seo DC, Macy JT, Hulvershorn LA, Kawata K. Association Between Head Impact Exposure, Psychological Needs, and Indicators of Mental Health Among U.S. High School Tackle Football Players. Journal of Adolescent Health. 2023;xxx:1–8.
abstract: This multisite, prospective cohort study investigated the effect of one season of head impact exposure (HIE) and age of first exposure to tackle football on acute mental health outcomes in 91 adolescent male football players (n=91) from four Midwestern high schools during the 2021 season. Sensor-installed mouthguards (Prevent Biometrics) were used to measure cumulative peak linear acceleration (PLA); mental health outcomes assessed included depressive symptoms (PHQ-9), anxiety symptoms (GAD-7), and thriving (BIT); and psychological need satisfaction was measured via the BPNSFS. A total of 9,428 head impacts were recorded with a mean of 102±113 impacts per player. Neither cumulative HIE nor age of first exposure was associated with postseason mental health outcomes or change scores from preseason to postseason. Greater psychological need satisfaction was significantly associated with fewer depressive symptoms, fewer anxiety symptoms, and greater thriving scores at postseason.
tldr: One season of head impact exposure and earlier age of first exposure to tackle football were not associated with worse acute mental health outcomes in high school players; however, psychological need satisfaction (autonomy, competence, relatedness) consistently predicted better mental health, underscoring the importance of psychosocial factors in adolescent athlete well-being.
methods: Multisite prospective observational cohort study; 2021 high school football season; n=91 male players from four Midwestern high schools; sensor-installed mouthguards (Prevent Biometrics Impact Monitor) for head impact measurement at 3.2 kHz; film validation of 19% of impacts (94% agreement); mental health surveys at preseason (July) and postseason (November); PHQ-9 for depressive symptoms, GAD-7 for anxiety, 10-item Brief Inventory of Thriving (BIT), Basic Psychological Needs Satisfaction and Frustration Scale (BPNSFS); linear regression models; Bonferroni-corrected significance thresholds (p<.008 for postseason scores; p<.017 for change scores); age and school used as control variables.
findings:
• 9,428 total head impacts recorded; mean 102±113 impacts per player
• Season-long cumulative PLA: 1,652±1,895 g; cumulative PRA: 117,602±145,014 rad/s²
• Neither cumulative HIE nor age of first exposure predicted postseason depression, anxiety, or thriving scores
• Cumulative HIE did not predict changes in mental health from preseason to postseason
• Greater psychological need satisfaction predicted fewer depressive symptoms (β=−0.035, p<.001), fewer anxiety symptoms (β=−0.021, p=.010), and greater thriving (β=0.278, p<.001) at postseason
• Effect sizes for psychological need satisfaction associations were moderate to large (R²=0.18–0.45)
• 4.4% of participants scored ≥10 on PHQ-9 at preseason; 5.5% at postseason
limitations: Predominantly White and non-Hispanic sample from Midwestern schools limits generalizability; single-season follow-up (~16 weeks) may be too short to detect neuropsychological changes; self-reported age of first exposure is subject to recall bias; male-only sample; external factors (team culture, win-loss records) not captured; study underpowered for some models; does not address chronic or long-term head impact effects.
practicalImplications: Programs should prioritize psychosocial support and psychological need satisfaction (autonomy, competence, relatedness) for adolescent football players as a strategy to promote positive mental health. A single season of head impacts does not appear to acutely worsen mental health, but multiyear longitudinal studies are needed. Coaches and staff should create environments that fulfill athletes' basic psychological needs to support mental well-being.
athleteDev: Directly applicable to American high school football. Findings suggest that the mental health of adolescent football players during a single season is more strongly influenced by psychosocial factors (psychological need satisfaction) than by the number or magnitude of head impacts sustained. This has implications for how programs structure team culture, athlete autonomy, and coach-athlete relationships to support player development and well-being.
rtp: The lack of acute mental health deterioration attributable to single-season HIE provides some reassurance for RTP decisions in adolescent players. However, clinicians should still screen for psychosocial factors such as psychological need satisfaction during RTP evaluation, as these predict mental health outcomes independent of head impact burden.

citation: Stemper BD, Harezlak J, Shah AS, Rowson S, Mihalik JP, Riggen L, Duma S, Pasquina P, Broglio SP, McAllister TW, McCrea MA, and CARE Consortium Investigators. Association Between Preseason/Regular Season Head Impact Exposure and Concussion Incidence in NCAA Football. Medicine & Science in Sports & Exercise. 2022.
abstract: This study used data from 1,120 athlete-seasons across six NCAA Division I football programs over five years (2015–2019) from the CARE Consortium Advanced Research Core (ARC) to examine associations between head impact exposure (HIE) and concussion incidence during different periods of the college football season. Head Impact Telemetry (HIT) System data were collected in real time, and concussions were diagnosed by team medical staff using DoD/AAN consensus definitions. The analysis quantified HIE during preseason, regular season, and total season periods using cumulative impact count and risk-weighted exposure (RWE) metrics, then related these to concussion incidence during those respective periods. Strong associations were identified between preseason HIE and both preseason and total season concussion incidence, suggesting a prolonged effect of preseason HIE on concussion risk extending throughout the full fall season.
tldr: Elevated head impact exposure during the college football preseason is associated with increased concussion risk not only during the preseason but throughout the entire fall season, suggesting that preseason HIE may reduce an athlete's concussion tolerance in a sustained manner—making preseason load management a critical injury prevention target.
methods: Retrospective analysis of prospectively collected CARE Consortium ARC data; 1,120 athlete-seasons from six NCAA Division I programs; 2015–2019 fall seasons; Head Impact Telemetry (HIT) System for real-time HIE measurement; concussion incidence monitored by team medical staff per CARE/DoD/AAN protocol; HIE quantified as cumulative impact count and risk-weighted exposure (RWE) summing individual concussion risk functions; periods analyzed: preseason, regular season, total season; associations between HIE in one period and concussion incidence in same and subsequent periods analyzed; IRB approved at Medical College of Wisconsin with reliance agreements at all sites.
findings:
• Preseason HIE was significantly associated with preseason concussion incidence
• Preseason HIE was also significantly associated with total season concussion incidence
• Total season HIE was significantly associated with total season concussion incidence
• NCAA Division I football athletes sustained a median of 426 impacts (75th percentile: 754) over a full season in prior related work
• Preseason HIE occurs at approximately twice the proportion of regular season HIE
• Individual preseason HIE was associated with season-long HIE, consistent with individual playing-style differences persisting across a season
• Findings provide first evidence for a sustained, season-long reduction in concussion tolerance associated with elevated preseason HIE
limitations: Observational design precludes causal inference; HIT System limited to specific Riddell helmet models; variability in playing time not fully controlled; concussion diagnosis dependent on athlete self-report and clinical recognition (subject to underreporting); RWE metric based on biomechanical risk functions not validated for all athlete populations; limited to six Division I programs, limiting generalizability to other competitive levels.
practicalImplications: Preseason practice structure is a critical lever for managing season-long concussion risk in college football. Reducing head impact exposure during preseason—through rule changes (e.g., eliminating two-a-day practices, already adopted by the NCAA), modified contact drills, and practice session management—may have protective effects extending throughout the entire competitive season. Sports medicine and S&C staff should treat preseason HIE load management as a primary injury prevention strategy.
athleteDev: Directly applicable to American college football. The finding that preseason HIE predicts season-long concussion incidence has major implications for how programs structure preseason training. Individual athletes with consistently high playing-style-driven HIE across preseason and regular season may represent a chronically elevated-risk population warranting individualized monitoring and intervention.
rtp: The concept of a sustained reduction in concussion tolerance following elevated HIE is directly relevant to RTP protocols. Athletes returning from concussion during a high-HIE preseason may face elevated re-injury risk for the remainder of the season. Clinicians should consider cumulative preseason HIE as a contextual risk factor when making RTP decisions and may benefit from monitoring ongoing HIE load post-clearance.

citation: Aita SL, Muchintala R, Suresh A, Patel S, Schuler B, Lichtenstein JD. Association of Use of a Mobile Tackling Dummy During College Football Practice With Reduced Sport-Related Concussion: Results of a Pilot Investigation. The Orthopaedic Journal of Sports Medicine. 2024;12(2):23259671241228316.
abstract: This descriptive epidemiology pilot study investigated the association between two primary prevention strategies—no-tackle practices and mobile tackling dummy use—and sport-related concussion (SRC) frequency across 14 seasons (2007–2019, 2021) for Dartmouth College and 7 seasons (2013–2019) for seven other Ivy League football teams. SRC data were analyzed across total, preseason, in-season, in-practice, and in-game settings using correlations, generalized linear models, and basic descriptive statistics. There was a statistically significant downward trend in in-game SRCs for Dartmouth over the seasons studied (r=−0.52; P=.029). In multivariable models, mobile tackling dummy use was independently associated with lower total (β=−0.53; P=.049), in-season (β=−0.63; P=.023), and in-game (β=−0.79; P=.003) SRCs, while no-tackle practice alone did not reach significance. These findings suggest that mobile tackling dummy use during practice may be an effective primary prevention tool for reducing concussions in college football.
tldr: Adding a mobile tackling dummy (MVP/Drive) to no-tackle practice culture was independently associated with significant reductions in in-season and in-game concussions at Dartmouth College and trended toward lower SRC frequency in other Ivy League teams, suggesting this technology is a promising primary concussion prevention strategy for college football.
methods: Descriptive epidemiology pilot study; Dartmouth College men's football (n=14 seasons: 2007–2019 and 2021, excluding 2020 due to COVID-19) and 7 other Ivy League men's football teams (n=7 seasons: 2013–2019); SRC data from Dartmouth College Sports Medicine Department and Ivy League–Big Ten Epidemiology of Concussion Study; SRCs diagnosed by sports medicine professionals using Zurich or Berlin consensus criteria; primary prevention policies: no-tackle practice (Dartmouth from 2010; other Ivy League from 2016) and mobile tackling dummy use (Dartmouth from 2015); bivariate correlations for temporal trends; generalized linear models for independent associations of practice policies with SRC frequency; Tukey post hoc pairwise comparisons; 1-tailed P values for hypothesized directional effects (α=.05 for all).
findings:
• Statistically significant downward trend in in-game SRCs for Dartmouth over seasons studied (r=−0.52; P=.029)
• Mobile tackling dummy independently associated with lower total SRCs (β=−0.53; P=.049), in-season SRCs (β=−0.63; P=.023), and in-game SRCs (β=−0.79; P=.003)
• No-tackle practice alone was not significantly associated with lower SRCs in any model at Dartmouth
• Post hoc comparisons: seasons with −tackling/+dummies had significantly fewer in-season (P=.032) and in-game (P=.004) SRCs vs. −tackling/−dummies
• Other Ivy League teams: no-tackle policy seasons associated with fewer total SRCs (mean 52.3 vs. 70.7; d=1.17), though not statistically significant due to limited power
• Effect sizes were robust (η²=0.24–0.51; d/g=0.83–1.99) despite limited statistical power
• Dartmouth's team performance did not worsen with adoption of no-tackle/dummy policies
limitations: Small sample size (n=14 seasons for Dartmouth; n=7 for other Ivy League schools) limits statistical power; observational/correlational design precludes causal inference; no data on mobile tackling dummy use from other Ivy League schools; unidentified confounders (coaching changes, roster size, referee enforcement, team culture) may influence SRC frequency; SRC underreporting is a pervasive concern in athletics; findings may not generalize beyond Ivy League to other conferences or competitive levels.
practicalImplications: Implementing mobile tackling dummy technology (e.g., MVP/Drive by MVP Robotics) in practice—particularly in conjunction with a no-tackle practice culture—may be a meaningful primary prevention strategy for reducing concussions during games and across the season. Programs considering adoption should view this within a broader team culture emphasizing player safety. The technology appears to transfer safe tackling mechanics to game situations, reducing concussion frequency without apparent performance cost.
athleteDev: Directly applicable to American college football. The mobile tackling dummy allows athletes to practice full tackling mechanics against a moving target without player-on-player contact, maintaining skill development while reducing cumulative head impact exposure during practice. Programs can integrate this tool into daily practice structures to develop safe and effective tackling technique that transfers to game performance.
rtp: While this study focuses on primary prevention rather than RTP, the findings are relevant for athletes managing concussion history. Programs using no-tackle and mobile dummy practices reduce overall practice-related head impact exposure, which may lower re-injury risk for athletes who have recently returned to full participation following concussion. These practice modifications represent a structural RTP consideration for high-risk athletes.

citation: Liu Z, Dudley JA, Diekfuss JA, Ahmed N, Edmondson AD, Cecil KM, Yuan W, Zuleger TM, Slutsky-Ganesh AB, Barber Foss KD, Myer GD, Fleischer CC. Associations Between Brain Metabolites Measured With MR Spectroscopy and Head Impacts in High School American Football Athletes. Journal of Magnetic Resonance Imaging. 2024.
abstract: This prospective controlled trial used proton MR spectroscopy (¹H MRS) to examine changes in brain metabolites—total N-acetylaspartate (tNAA), total choline (tCho), myo-inositol (myol), and glutamate+glutamine (Glx)—in the left primary motor cortex (M1) and anterior cingulate cortex (ACC) of 215 male high school American football players (ages 13–18) from seven Cincinnati-area schools during the 2018 season. Participants were divided into a jugular vein compression (JVC) collar group (n=114) and noncollar group (n=101). Head impacts were tracked via accelerometers affixed to the mastoid process; MRS was acquired pre- and postseason, with an additional postconcussion scan for the 24 athletes who sustained a concussion. In non-concussed participants, significant decreases in tCho/tCr in ACC and increases in Glx/tCr in ACC were observed from pre- to postseason. The relationship between ΔtCho/tCr and mean g-force of impacts differed significantly between participants with and without concussion at g-force thresholds above 80 g, and use of a JVC collar was associated with distinct myol/tCr trends in the ACC at higher g-force thresholds.
tldr: Repeated subconcussive head impacts during a high school football season produce measurable neurochemical changes in the brain as detected by MRS, with tCho/tCr in the primary motor cortex showing a significant positive association with mean g-force of impacts in concussed players, suggesting that MRS metabolite changes—particularly involving membrane turnover markers—may be sensitive indicators of cumulative head impact burden even without diagnosed concussion.
methods: Prospective controlled trial; n=284 recruited, 215 included in final analysis (mean age 15.9±1.0 years); seven greater Cincinnati area high schools; 2018 competitive season (August–November); JVC collar group (n=114) wore Q-collar (Q30 Innovations) during all practices and games; head impact tracking via CSx System accelerometer affixed to left mastoid; impacts >10 g triggered 45 ms recording windows; MRS acquired on 3 Tesla Philips MR scanners pre- and postseason with postconcussion scan where applicable; ¹H point resolved spectroscopy (PRESS) in M1 and ACC; metabolites: tNAA, tCho, myol, Glx normalized to tCr; MATLAB LCModel v6.3-I for spectral fitting; Wilcoxon signed-rank, Friedman's, and Mann–Whitney U tests for group comparisons; univariate linear regressions for metabolite–g-force relationships; FDR-corrected P-values; significance set at P≤0.05.
findings:
• In non-concussed participants: significant decrease in tCho/tCr in ACC (pre: 0.233±1.40×10⁻³; post: 0.227±1.47×10⁻⁷; FDR-corrected) and increase in Glx/tCr in ACC from pre- to postseason
• Relationship between ΔtCho/tCr and mean g-force of impacts differed significantly between concussed and non-concussed participants at g-force thresholds >80 g to >140 g in both M1 and ACC
• In concussed athletes: positive associations between ΔtCho/tCr in M1 and mean g-force >100 g (β=3.6×10⁻³; P=9.3×10⁻³) and >110 g (β=2.9×10⁻³; P=3.3×10⁻²)
• In the ACC, ΔtCho/tCr positively associated with mean g-force at thresholds >80 g to >140 g in concussed participants
• JVC collar associated with negative associations between ΔmyoI/tCr in ACC and mean g-force >110 g (β=−1.1×10⁻³; P=3.9×10⁻²) and >120 g (β=−1.1×10⁻³; P=2.0×10⁻²)
• No significant tNAA/tCr changes as a function of time, concussion, or collar use
• Median impacts >20 g per player: 296±20; 24 participants sustained a diagnosed concussion
limitations: Male-only sample from a single metropolitan area; relatively small concussion subgroup (n=24) with limited statistical power for concussion-specific analyses; accelerometer affixed to mastoid rather than helmet—may not precisely reflect head kinematics; single MRS voxel per region limits spatial coverage; metabolite changes are indirect markers of neurological change; no long-term follow-up beyond the competitive season; JVC collar assignment was not fully randomized across all teams.
practicalImplications: MRS-based brain metabolite markers—particularly tCho/tCr as an index of membrane turnover—show promise as objective, noninvasive biomarkers of cumulative head impact burden in adolescent athletes. The finding that neurochemical changes occur even without diagnosed concussion underscores the importance of subconcussive impact monitoring. The JVC collar's association with attenuated myoI/tCr changes at high g-force thresholds suggests potential neuroprotective benefit warranting further investigation.
athleteDev: Directly applicable to American high school football. Even without a diagnosed concussion, high school football players sustain neurochemical brain changes over a season proportional to the mean g-force of their head impacts. This finding reinforces the need for practice structure modifications that reduce high-magnitude head impacts (>80–110 g) to minimize cumulative neurobiological burden during adolescent development, a period of heightened brain vulnerability.
rtp: The neurochemical differences observed between concussed and non-concussed athletes in the relationship between tCho/tCr and head impact magnitude suggest that MRS biomarkers may eventually inform RTP decision-making by providing objective evidence of neurochemical recovery. Clinicians should be aware that postconcussion neurochemical trajectories differ from those of subconcussive impact accumulation, and that high-magnitude head impacts post-RTP may carry distinct neurobiological risk.
